hero.subtitle: Blending strategy, design, and technology to craft digital experiences that are intuitive, human, and results-driven.

projects.title: MusicSurf — Emotion-Driven Music Discovery
projects.description: Designing a new paradigm for music exploration — a network-based platform that organizes songs by mood, genre, and emotion rather than playlists. Full process from user interviews to high-fidelity prototype.

projects.description: Identifying and solving real-world pain points in a retail fulfilment system. Grounded in first-hand experience, this redesign prioritized efficiency, clarity, and ease of adoption for frontline employees.

aboutTeaser.bio: I hold a BA in Communication & Culture from the University of Calgary, with specializations in Computer Science and Visual Studies. I'm a freelancer and client success professional specializing in UX design, web design, and graphic design — with 3+ years of experience in tech and client-facing environments.

contact.subtitle: Have a project in mind or just want to say hello? I'd love to hear from you.

bio._: -- Bio paragraphs (array — each item is one paragraph) --
bio.heading: Hi, I'm Riyad.
paragraphs: I'm a UX designer, web designer, and marketing strategist based in Calgary, Alberta. I graduated from the University of Calgary with a Bachelor of Arts in Communication & Culture, specializing in Computer Science and Art & Art History — a combination that shaped how I think about design: through the lens of both logic and expression.

Currently, I work as a Client Success Specialist at ActiveDEMAND, where I help clients get the most out of a marketing automation platform — building landing pages, designing email workflows, and translating complex tools into simple, human experiences. Before that, I spent several years in retail, where I developed a deep appreciation for the people on the front lines of technology. That time directly inspired my Omni redesign project.

I believe the best digital products feel inevitable — like they couldn't have been designed any other way. My work sits at the intersection of empathy, strategy, and craft. Whether I'm mapping a user journey, wireframing an interface, or writing copy for a landing page, I'm always thinking about the person on the other end of the screen.
Outside of design, I'm drawn to music, illustration, and building things on the web just to see if I can.

cta.title: Want to collaborate?
cta.subtitle: I'm always open to interesting projects and conversations.

project1.title: MusicSurf
project1.subtitle: Designing an emotion-driven music discovery platform that lets users explore music like a forest — by mood, genre, and feeling.

problem.heading: The Problem
paragraphs: At the time, no platform allowed users to discover music based on how they actually feel. Spotify and Apple Music surfaced music through auto-generated playlists and algorithm-driven recommendations — with no transparency, no emotional context, and no real sense of exploration. Users weren't in control of how they found new music.

solution.heading: The Solution
paragraphs: MusicSurf is a concept platform that uses visualization to create a more natural music browsing experience. It maps important elements of songs — mood, speed, energy, genre — spatially in a network graph, so users can explore music the way you'd wander through a forest: intuitively, emotionally, and freely.

Instead of playlists and albums, songs are connected to each other by heuristics. Users can follow a path based on how they feel, or let the platform guide them. The result is discovery that feels personal — not algorithmic.

process.heading: Research & Process
process.intro: This was a full end-to-end UX project completed as part of my Human Computer Interaction I course at the University of Calgary. The process included:
steps: Stakeholder analysis and competitive review of existing music platforms
End-user interviews exploring how people discover and relate to music
Sketches and paper prototypes to explore the network graph interaction model
Low-fidelity wireframes for all core screens
High-fidelity prototype built in Adobe XD with full interaction flows
Peer and instructor critique sessions with iterated revisions

designDecisions.heading: Design Decisions
paragraphs: The colour palette draws from Spotify's dark-theme conventions, using a rich colour range within the network graph itself to communicate mood and genre — warm colours for energetic songs, cooler tones for ambient and melancholic tracks. This created an immediately readable visual language without requiring any text labels.

Particular attention was paid to the "Now Playing" experience — surfacing mood and energy data directly in the interface so users could understand why a song felt the way it did, and seamlessly continue their journey from there.

lessons.heading: Lessons Learned
paragraphs: Stakeholder and competitor analysis early in the process was invaluable — it sharpened the focus of the design and gave the team a clear point of difference to design toward. I also learned how to apply and respond to structured critique from peers and instructors, which became a critical part of how I refine work today.

If I rebuilt this project today, I'd spend more time on user testing with real participants and lean further into accessibility — especially around how colour is used to communicate emotion for users with colour blindness.

project2.title: Omni — Order Management Redesign
project2.subtitle: Redesigning a broken retail fulfilment tool from the inside out — driven by first-hand experience and a deep empathy for the people who use it every shift.

meta.role: UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing

problem.heading: The Problem
paragraphs: Working as a Fulfilment Associate at BuyBuy Baby, I used an order management system called Omni every shift. Over time, I noticed patterns in how my coworkers and I struggled with the same friction points — unclear shelf locations, cluttered order views, unintuitive workflows, and a general sense that the interface was designed for a database, not a person.

The tool got the job done, but it worked against us rather than with us. In retail, where speed and accuracy directly impact customer satisfaction, a poorly designed tool isn't just a frustration — it's a risk.

research.heading: Research & Discovery
research.intro: My research advantage here was unique: I was the user. Over months of daily use, I documented pain points, photographed problem areas, and informally interviewed coworkers about their biggest frustrations. Key insights:
steps: Shelf location data was buried and inconsistent, slowing item retrieval
Order information was spread across multiple screens, requiring excessive navigation
The packing and shipping workflow had unnecessary confirmation steps that added no value
New employees struggled with the learning curve — the system had no clear entry point
The visual design was dense and low-contrast, increasing cognitive load during busy shifts

solution.heading: The Redesign
paragraphs: The redesign focused on three core workflows: Prepare Shipments, Pack Shipments, and Completed Orders. Each was reconstructed with a cleaner information hierarchy, more contextual data visible at a glance, and a visual language using familiar BuyBuy Baby brand colours to reduce the jarring feel of a completely new interface.

I prioritised the principle of progressive disclosure — showing associates only what they need in each step of the workflow, and revealing additional information on demand rather than overwhelming upfront.

designDecisions.heading: Key Design Decisions
paragraphs: Clarity over density: The original interface packed too much information into every view. I restructured the layout to give each data point breathing room — making scanning faster and errors less likely under pressure.

Familiar branding, fresh UX: Using BuyBuy Baby's colour palette grounded the redesign in something associates already recognized, reducing cognitive resistance to change — a critical factor when redesigning tools for non-technical users who resist learning new systems.
Empathy-first prioritization: Every design decision was filtered through the question: does this make a tired associate's shift easier or harder?

lessons.heading: Lessons Learned
paragraphs: Being the user gave me incredible research depth, but it also created blind spots. I found myself assuming shared knowledge that newer employees didn't have. This reinforced how important inclusive design is — not just for accessibility, but for onboarding and experience-level diversity within the same user group.

I also learned that in enterprise and retail tooling, the best UX is often invisible: the goal is that associates don't have to think about the interface at all. They should be focused entirely on the customer.

links._: -- CTA buttons at bottom of case study --

project3.title: Humming Salad Bar
project3.subtitle: A playful, wordless interaction where your hum becomes your recipe — exploring sound as a non-traditional input modality in web design.

brief.heading: The Brief
paragraphs: For my Human Computer Interaction II course, the design challenge was deliberately unconventional: create a unique interaction using sound as input — without the use of words. No speech recognition. No commands. Just raw, human sound as a design material.

The constraint was the canvas. I explored several directions — a cat game operated by meows, a rhythm-based cricket game — before landing on the idea that felt most genuinely surprising and human: a salad bar that listens to you hum.

concept.heading: The Concept
paragraphs: The Humming Salad Bar is a mobile web app where users hum a tune into their microphone. Based on the characteristics of the hum — pitch, duration, pattern — the app generates a personalized salad bowl recipe. Users first set their dietary preferences (vegan, gluten-free, etc.), then hum away.

The delight is in the absurdity: the same song hummed at different pitches produces wildly different salads. It rewards curiosity and repeat interaction in a way that purely visual interfaces rarely do.

process.heading: Design Process
process.intro: I went through five distinct sketch phases before settling on the final layout — exploring different ways to visualize the listening state, the countdown before humming, and the recipe reveal. The full process:
steps: Ideation across three distinct interaction concepts
Sketch series: bowl generator concept, website layout, recipe panel, humming input states
Food preferences flow designed to be accessible and quick to complete
Custom Illustrator assets to reinforce the playful, hand-crafted visual identity
Built and deployed as a responsive mobile web app using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and Bootstrap

designDecisions.heading: Design Decisions
paragraphs: Tone through visuals: I chose a light green palette and hand-drawn Illustrator assets to signal playfulness immediately. The visual identity told users before they read a word: this is fun, this is weird, and that's the point.

The countdown state: One of the trickier design problems was the humming countdown screen — letting users know they needed to hum without instructions. I landed on an animated countdown with a microphone visual that listened, giving just enough non-verbal feedback to guide the interaction.

Dietary preferences first: Allowing users to set preferences before humming gave them a sense of agency and personalization — the result felt tailored rather than random. This was key to making repeat use feel worthwhile.

lessons.heading: Lessons Learned
paragraphs: The biggest tension in this project was between randomness and meaning. Too much randomness and the app felt like a novelty with no reason to return. Too little, and the connection between humming and salad felt arbitrary. I learned that even in playful, experimental UX, users need to feel like their input matters — that they have some legible agency over the outcome.

I also learned to embrace constraints as a creative force. The 'no words' rule pushed me into territory I wouldn't have explored otherwise, and the result was far more interesting than a conventional recipe generator would have been.
